speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Sly just come out and give himself up or did the people go up and reason with him? Do you remember about that?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't remember just exactly how that was. But I think it, I really think that he gave himself up, is what I think. Because if he hadn't, there wouldn't have been anybody going up there against that rifle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the business there with his wife and daughter, was that a real fight?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know. I didn't see it, but I had my guesses. I kind of thought that that was put on to get him up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you standing when this was going on in town? Where were you?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I was down there, but there used to be a fruit house there. I don't know, there isn't anything there anymore. Down there by the railroad track, there used to be a fruit house there. Now I'm just standing out from the edge of that. Down there by the depot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was Hayes, where was Hayes when he heard the fight between the breaking out with the girl screaming?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know where he was when he heard it, but he came running up there anyway.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And, you know, Phil had not said anything that they heard, just what you see.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, but even so, most of the people that talked about it, they're all gone now. So what they said, what you heard, I mean, that's important too. What people said, you can say it's not what you said, what you saw, but what you heard.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I heard that this slide and Hayes had had his argument uptown. And this slide told him, I said, I'll get you. I've heard that. But I didn't hear it myself, I just heard that people said that.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: This Hayes was always a shooting around town.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Didn't seem like he was always kind of a trigger happy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You liked to fire his gun?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah. He just seemed like he kind of liked to use a gun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any times when he pushed people around?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, he tried to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or hearing about times? What? Or hearing about times when he did?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, there was one time they had an ordinance there in town that you couldn't ride in a drive faster than a walk through Main Street. And he was out there and grabbed everybody he could. And there was one guy that was going down the street. He had an old scrappy bridle on his horse that didn't amount to anything. He was riding a little too fast and old Hayes come out there and stop him, grabbed a hold of bridle. And old Hayes slipped it over the horse, er, the guy slipped it over. Don't remember who it was. But he slipped the bridle off over the horse's head and so dispersed the horse and run over the top of the old bad Hayes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Come pretty near what?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Come pretty near getting him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He went down in the mud, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: He was always looking for something like that, you know. And he'd holler and he was always shooting. One time my aunt thought that she got scared. She thought she was going to get hit. She was going up the street from the old man that started shooting. He was shooting at somebody. I don't know who it was now. He was a character, that guy.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I got one more question about that business, the shootout there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far above the railroad depot was Sly's house?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know just exactly. Well, the house, I think, is still there. It's a house sitting up on the hillside there. That used to be outside of the city limits.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And it was at that time. It was outside the city limits.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that Hayes had no right to go out there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know whether he did or not.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't know what the rulings was on that family.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: But this... I always figured that Sly was a pretty decent sort of a guy. But you know, a lot of people can be pushed just a little too far.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: You know that.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: You told me that there were a couple of times that Hayes had run -ins with Joe Wells.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yes, he did. Well, he didn't just... Well, he did have a run -in, too. The time that he came out there to get that Benson that used to live up there, he came out to get him. And this guy saw... This Benson saw him coming, and he slipped out and came up to Joe Wells' place. And Hayes knew that they were good friends, so he just took a hunch that this Benson was up there, and he came up to get him.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And Joe Wells told him... He was going to come in and search the place to get him. Joe Wells said, you've got to search for it.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: He didn't have to have one or something similar to that. He was going to come in. But when this Benson came up there, Joe Wells gave the boys and his wife a rifle apiece and had a place where he wanted them. And he told the old man, Hayes, that he wasn't going to come in there.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And they were waiting for him. If he had tried to come in there, he'd get there and would have shot him.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And another time, this Benson and him was in town.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Benson and Joe Wells. Yeah.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And they'd been drinking a little bit. They drank pretty heavy, both of them. And they got ready and started to go home. And his other horses were going home, and the old man, Hayes, jumped onto his horse and took after him. Got out there and he got started. One of them stopped and started shooting. And Benson got scared and stopped. Old Joe Wells was laying over on the side of his horse, heading for home. But when Benson quit and went back, well, he went back too and made old Hayes turn loose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He made old Hayes turn loose, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I wonder how he did that. I don't know.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Old Joe Wells had the kind of way of when he said things, they could tell that he meant it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Benson? Are you talking about John Benson?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't know what his given name was. He lived up there, Deary, up there, down over the hill there from where Joe Wells lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was Joe Wells?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, my way of booking him, he was a first -class guy. He was a good guy.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And he had been a slave one time. He was freed. His old master gave him the name of Wells. That was his name. He gave it to Joe Wells. The name was Wells. And then there was, in a matter of years, there was a couple of those Wells boys, lived up there by Deary. And old Joe Wells and them were great friends. Bitch sit back and forth all the time.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Old Hayes had a habit of drinking with guys until he'd get him drink a little bit, and then he'd rest him for being drunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Some of them asked him if he, when he went to the restroom, what's the matter, you need another sack of flour?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: He was drinking with old Dick Shandler on there one time, this old one, when they had those long -handled goblets. And the old man had drank with him ever since he'd been in there, all he'd drank, and then he was going to rest him for being drunk. And old Dick Shandler, when he hit him over the head with one of those goblets, knocked him down, and he'd drug him outside. There was a living party right next to it, and they had a well there. And he took the lid off the well and shoved the old man down in there, and hold his head under. It was just about drowned, and he just pulled him off.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: It sounds like for Hayes being such a tough guy, it sounds like he got his quite a bit.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, there's some tough guys around Troy too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear tell of times when Hayes really beat up people, Marshall Hayes himself did?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know if I ever heard of him beating anybody. I think he's too big a coward for that. He'd shoot him if he got a chance.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: So that's the kind of a guy he was. That's why you don't hear much about him taking on people, because you didn't think he could take them unless he shot them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about Lou Wells, Joe Wells' wife? What do you remember about her?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't remember very much about her in a way. Well, in a way too. They had sheep up there, and she would take that wool and wash that wool, card it, make yarn, and knit socks for men. She'd knit them great big and then shrink them, and she'd sell them for 50 cents a pair out of that wool, pure wool. I don't know. I think you should get a poured water and no socks in it to handle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I turn this down a little bit, this heater?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah. Well, no. Here, over there is a place to turn it down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why is it too warm in here, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, just pull it over toward you.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: That'll cool her down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that she liked to beat on her washtub?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, it was a dishpan. You know, they didn't have waterworks up there then, and they just had the water, and then they'd go out and throw the water out, you see. And she'd come back and beat on the bottom of the dishpan. And there was a fellow with me up there when I was driving that mail route by the name of Marvin Froman, and she'd come back and beat on the bottom of the dishpan, and he got up and started to step on her off in the middle of the floor up there. She got to laugh at it. She'd pound it on that dishpan, and she'd put her not to all the skin off her fingers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Laugh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: She's laughing every minute of the time.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: She laughed a lot.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: But when she was beating on that dishpan, she was laughing when he was stepping her off out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he started to dance, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, he was jigging, you know.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And she was giving the time to beat for it, you know, and he was just having the time, and so was she.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear—did you ever see her chewing tobacco?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, I never noticed her doing that. I guess she did, though, with all those Southern women did. I don't know for sure about her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the house—what their house looked like, what the house was like?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, the log house.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Built the logs, you know. The old fashion way of building it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was also a lodging house, right?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't think that they stayed there for a long time. They were there at night so much, but, you see, they always served meals there at noon, I know, because when I was driving that mail route up there, that was our halfway house. We stopped there for dinner every day, going and coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have many people there for the noon meal, or just be just you?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, everybody that's riding the mail route was me. See, all passengers, too, and they all got to stop there to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what would one of the lunches be? What would you have for lunch there? Be very big?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, it would have all we could eat, that's all. I could say I couldn't remember just what it was. It was good cooking, too.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, tell me some about that old mail route that you had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did it go?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: It went from Troy to Collins. You put out, well, the first stop was there at Nora. You know, there used to be a kind of store and a post office there at Nora. And then we went on from Nora, and we went to, I think the next stop was at Avon, and I had those little leather bags to, the oil cloth bags to carry the mail, and they had a place up there where I could hang them on as I went by.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And we went to Avon. We were out there, and then we went to Bear Creek, and then we went north up to the Avon, and there's a farmhouse up there where the post office was. And then we came back, and then Anderson right down there at Bear Creek, right where there's bridges across Bear Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where that old barn is sitting there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, there used to be a store building there, and that used to be the post office at Anderson. And then from Anderson, we went to, I went to Janesville, but on the road before I got there is where we stopped to eat dinner at Joe Wells. And we went to Janesville, and I had to come back a little ways, and then the next stop after Janesville was Collins, the next post office. But all along the road, everybody along the road, they had their oil cloth sack. I had to leave their mail for them all the way along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they'd leave that sack right by the roadside there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, they'd have a whole little rig fixed up where you'd hang it on. And that's all the way up there. That's the way old Wovell used to get his mail to. There was no post office there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have their name written on the sack, or did you just know who it was?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, they had their name written on the sack as a rule, but I knew just about who it was anyhow from the sack.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Old Abe Frye run the post office at Janesville, or not Janesville, but Collins. And the father to those Lawrence who's there at Deary run the Janesville post office. And I don't know who run the— Well, Anderson, I guess the name of Anderson, run the Anderson post office. But I can't remember the one that run the ones at Avon. I don't remember what their names was, nor the one at Norah. I don't remember who run that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the road like to travel on?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Terrible.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Of course there was no gravel at that time at all. In places where we'd put gravel, now they had a corduroy. Where it was soft and muddy, you know, they'd cut poles and lay them crossways of the road. And it bumpity -bump, bump, bump, bump, all over that. It was just an awful lot of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they laid down just one next to another?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, I was just laying right side to side. You can remember what that would be like. You didn't get up any speed limits or anything like that. It was a walk there, sometimes pretty slow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it very muddy?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Sometimes.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Someplace where it was kind of swampy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No places you had to swim, though, were there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, not when I was driving. But there was times in the winter when I was only there in the summertime during the school vacation. And in the wintertime, sometimes I guess they had to leave the rig and take the horse and swim the cricks and everything else to get up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the roads follow close to the way they are now or were they no different?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: They are different, quite a bit different from where they are now. There ain't very many places where it goes anyway very close to it. No, when we got out there, when we went over to go to Avon, we went over there about to Dry Creek and then took off. There used to be a sawmill back up there. We would go in there and go across and then go down to Barrick Creek from there. And it wasn't any way near where the road is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How wide was it? Was it wide enough that two rigs could pass each other on it?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, if you looked for the right spot, it was. It was mainly just a single road. But you know you can put your own pull -offs. It doesn't have anything to buy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you run into many people using the road besides yourself?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, quite a few sometimes. Sometimes there wasn't so many. Sometimes you wouldn't see anybody hardly from the time you left until you got back.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I think how different that country was.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, I'll tell you that there at Wovell, that was the prettiest place I ever saw. The first time I went up there, I thought we could go in there just in the evening, you know. And there was those big meadows. There was meadows there and those big tall trees that you just look way back to the seat at the top of them. And the cattle was coming in there, bawling, you know. And I thought that was pretty. But they sure spotted the looks of that country.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Those little white pine trees, they were tall.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: There was a natural meadow there at Wovell. Yeah.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And then there was a natural meadow there at Collins too. There were several little meadows up along that creek that goes up there. Several of them. I think that's the south fork, or the west fork of the potlatch. Right up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Wovell, was he the only person right there at the town site at that time?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: He was the only one there then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you were telling me too that when you got up there at the place that you stayed overnight?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, yes, I stayed there at Collins overnight. Then left there in the morning and came back again.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: That's up there where they had Driscoll's, who got their cow corral up there now. They got all painted white up there on the side of Wovell, there on the meadow. That used to be the old fry place, the even fry place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you stay at Collins?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I stayed at people by the name of Clark. They had a cabin there close. That's where I ate, and I slept in the barn.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: A little long barn. He had a cougar's collar once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many of the people around there out that far at that time?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, there seemed to be, there at Collins, there seemed to be more people live there, and there wasn't any place else that is close together there. That was the end of the road. From there on, if you wanted to go any further, you had to go horseback. There were a lot of them that came up there and took their horses and packs and went back to their timber claims back in the mountains, back in around Clarky and on back up in there. That's as far as the road went. I owned a dare. It was one of them that had a claim on back up. She went up there, went horseback to it, back up in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're telling me there were quite a few girls out there during the summers, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, yes. Dr. Dares, that and their friends.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, he had a good time up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what time would you get in in the afternoon on a trip?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, you'd get in there just about dark, just a little before dark.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I imagine in the wintertime the guys didn't make it before dark.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Because they didn't have as long a day of it as I did.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: So you'd get in there and you'd leave here. You'd put the horses in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there somebody to take care of in there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, I'd take care of myself.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I had the barn there and I'd get the feed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girls could not meet you there?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: The one that I had the most fun with was Carrie McConnell.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: She was Bush's wife up there in Moscow. She was full of fun. I had a lot of fun with her.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Her and Lula there were pretty good friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how old were you then at that time?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: About fifteen.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I've had fun all my life. They didn't have dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there anything out there then, did they?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: No, not that's all I was out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the kids did to entertain themselves, to keep themselves busy during the summer?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't know if there's up there. It's a nice place up there in the woods along that meadow stream and run up in there. There's a lot of fishing going on. Lots of good fishing there. And of course you get a bunch of kids together, they can always entertain yourselves. You know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of hack did you have? Did you use?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, it's what we call the hack that was a two -seated hack.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: There were two horses on it.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: About all there is to tell about it, I guess.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I'm sort of curious too about what was going on along that route.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much clearing up the land going on then? Was there much logging?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Not so much. No, there was no logging at all. And they just, if they wanted to clear up some ground for putting in hay, they just cut the timber down and burned it. Many thousands of feet of good salt timber was just burned up to get rid of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, at the time you went up through there, was there very much of it had been cleared even alongside the road?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Not very much.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Just little patches. And that's where Derry sits now. Out in there, those are kind of a, well, they had Timothy in there and those big trees, they'd girded those big trees and killed them. They were standing there, there were white trees out there. Timothy, they raised Timothy in around there, they cut that Timothy himself.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: That's where Derry is sitting now. The road went up there where the schoolhouse is. That's where the road went through. It went right on straight through, you can see where it's there now, where the road goes through there. That's the way we went through. But then they got the road on this other side. Now, the main road, and that road went right up through past Joe Well's place and on down over the hill to Benson's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were these people making getting by just by doing a little bit of farming at that time?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know what they were doing. I think the most of them was getting by that way.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: They'd clear up a little patch of ground, have some Timothy and they'd sell it. They'd have some cattle that they'd sell it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this business about Norah being a tough place?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I've heard that a couple of places.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't know anything about that, about Norah being tough. It used to be a sawmill right there in Norah. It's called the Pawn Mill. It's just out this way from where Norah is right out there, and that little clearing out there. It used to be a sawmill there. When you get down that close, where there's quite a little sawmills and things like that. And that old Dalberg used to have a sawmill out. A little west of Well's down there, right down there where the road used to make that turn and go across the bridge. His sawmill was right down in Well's there, but I never saw it running very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that the Dalberg girl once stopped to fight?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that story?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, it was a drunken brawl. A couple of guys got into kind of a fight, and one of them was going to shoot the other, and the Dalberg girl put her hand down in front of the hammer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was a lot of fighting going on in Troy?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Because it was a, I mean I've heard that it was a lumber town, a tough town around the turn of the century. Well, there used to be about a year average being killed there.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: But I wasn't around to that part of it very much. But I was here about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Joe Welles had a real, did he have a reputation for being a tough guy, or was it just a guy not to rile?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, he didn't have any tough name. He was a good guy. I never heard of him being tough.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: In the latter years he had a lobbying camp up in there. I don't know much about that. I just saw a picture of it one time. Where he had his lobbying going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you send him, when we were talking about him before, that he was as white a man as you ever saw?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, he was. He was white inside. If he was your friend, he was your friend. All the way. You know, you can't judge a man from the looks of his skin on the outside. That's what he is inside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any other guys that stand out to you like him or Marshall Hayes or people that are really real characters?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I don't know what you would call him. Joe Welles was a character. I always figured he was a good guy. Not as a character.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I didn't mean that as something bad at all. I just meant as a character somebody that stood out.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And I think Joe did from what I was. He did. There was Swedish and Norwegian getting a fight up there and they'd say, well, Joe Welles is the only white man who was in the country.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I've never heard of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fire in the saloon?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, that was a set fire. And it fell by the name of Knudsen. It had that building rented from old Charlie Reitman. It had his saloon in there. I don't know what the deal was, but this Knudsen hired Phil the name of Henning to set fire there to burn it out to get his insurance on his stock that he had there.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Old Henning gave up and he'd come around to old Charlie Reitman and wanted to shake hands with him. Old Charlie Reitman told him to shake hands with yourself. He was down there and killed Phoebe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Phoebe?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah. His wife's name was Fanny and he called her Phoebe. He didn't want anything to do with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that one come out? Do you know that he'd have done that?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, I think both of them went to Penn for it. I don't know for sure. I didn't always learn all that kind of stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about fruit growing around the town of Troy? You were telling me last time that that was a pretty big operation for a while, huh?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yes, it was.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Up on top of the hill, going out toward the ridges up there toward Kendrick, that whole thing up there was orchards up on top of the hill there. They're farming it now, but before, there's into orchards that, I don't know just how far it went. But then even down on the ridge, down on Driscoll Ridge, the people down there had a lot of orchards. And they'd bring all the fruit in there to Troy and stored it there at their warehouse, fruit houses that they had built there.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: There was just a lot of it. There was a lot of cherries, too. Cherries, peach flums, pears, apples.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think they went to mostly? This stuff?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: The prunes, I don't think. They dried them and shipped the dried fruit. They came down there by the Idaho Bean Elevator Company. There used to be a prune dryer there. And the Nazarene Church used to be a prune dryer. I know that Johansson out north there, he had a prune dryer out there. And then there was some more. Well, there was one down on the ridge, down toward Kendrick that I know of. And then there was two or three of them up in north of what used to be Cornwall. There was some of them up in there that I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think happened that all ended?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Well, they got so they could make more money and raise more wheat than they could raise the fruit, I think. And then they had to do so much spray up to get rid of those worms, you know. And it was extra work. You know, people don't like to work too well. So they did something that was easier.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the seasons pretty hard on it sometimes? Did they have failures very often?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I don't know about that.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Because I know even now in Lewiston they got failures with the peaches sometimes. Oh, I know. But peaches and apples are two different things.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: And those prunes, it takes quite a lot to kill the prunes, the crop. The way I remembered it, I got some prune trees out here in my backyard now. They take quite a bit of beating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that your father was involved with that warehouse business for a while?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yes.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: My uncle Will Deafy and him was in partnership there for a while. They were brother -in -laws, you know. You know how brother -in -laws get along.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, they had to run the warehouse there where they had the fruit.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: We used to pack cherries there too to ship their Troy. You know, what they'll do for a fella when they keep eating them all the time.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I packed cherries, made cherry boxes, and nailed them up.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: There's some people I would like to know where they are. That's the easy girls that used to work there packing fruit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the family leave the country?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Yeah, they've gone somewhere. I can't find anybody that knows anything about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your folks come to come out anyway? Where did they come from?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: They come from South Dakota. I don't know. South Dakota was kind of tough. They were hunting for a better place. They found it. My dad and my mother's folks and Deafy, they all come out about the same time. All together, you might say, I guess. They brought me out here before I was a year old. That's a good while to go.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: They used to be quite a lot of fruit raised down on the Driscoll's Ridge down on the lower end. A lot of it comes from up from down there.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: I suppose that fruit was going real good before they had a lot of their land cleared, too. Before they got the fruit down pretty good until the worms got started to come into bed. Then they had to be so careful all they'd raise were worms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the old threshing fires they used to have? They threshing when you were here and you were young?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Out on the ridges. I know they hadn't really watched that for a while.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, yes. There was a good many of those down in there. Mostly when I was with the threshing, I went out to Indicott. I went out there and worked in that headed drain. Drove dairy for the threshing outfits when I was about 14.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you get to go back very much to Troy and that area?

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: Oh, I go up there fishing. That's where I do most of the fishing up there, that Spring Valley Reservoir. You know where that is. That's where they share it.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: You know, they are not sure of the relation to mine. There, Troy.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: There used to be some deathies around there, but they're not there anymore.

speaker: Glenn Corrin
words: My uncle went to Lewiston and they had that seed outfit down there at Lewiston, Deathy Seed Company. He was in the bank there for a while at Clarkston too, I think.
